item.title: We are a nation of immigrants
item.description: I’d trade now Captain Idrache for any or all of these lying, treasonous RedHats and Fat Nixon as well. We are a nation of immigrants, a truth to be embraced not reviled. https://www.dvidshub.net/news/199082/port-au-prince-west-point-maryland-guards-first-graduate Captain Idrache as of 2021. https://www.southcom.mil/MEDIA/NEWS-ARTICLES/Article/2783312/haitian-born-black-hawk-pilot-supports-jtf-haiti-earthquake-relief-efforts/

item.title: It's time for yet another unhinged XL Wednesday CeleryDump for the good & bad people of Imgur
item.description:
greetings online friends
nutritious
wait what
and?
almost as if these places were named long ago
no weapons
that's unsettling
please
it's nice that there are only 3 types
hi bob
my dudes
what could go wrong?
sure why not
endless entertainment
dangit Superman
obviously
thanks T
that's my time.. have a great night gang!!

Here's my #wallpaper for the sake of the theme..

But you can catch Deaf Cat Magoo (short for Oogala Magoogala) on https://twitch.tv/bottomshelfvodka

item.title: Oopsies, Ouchies, & Oh Shits! #4 (Weak Willed be Warned)
item.description:
Oopsies
Ouchies
Oh shit!
Welcome to Triple-O, how tough are ya?
If you find that this is your limit then please consider moving to the next post (:
But if you’re still here, then let’s ride!
Take this fuel comrade. You’re going to need it.
MESS WITH COW GET THE OW
Would 100% turn a tent into a portapotty.
Bro heard Grandma say “Hit the gas” and accelerated.
YOU HAD ONE JOB Lmao I just noticed the inflatable says “Fat Boys”
You’d think the weight of his massive balls would throw off his equilibrium.
*dink*
Quentin?
Wear a helmet <3
Guess the “H” stands for “Hell yeah” cause that is clearly Jesus H. Christ.
I bet he said “hold my beer” moments earlier.
*tries to shoot web*
How much could it have hurt? It was a soft drink.
Back to Back World War Champs, ‘Murica!
*windows shut down noise*
This is just tearable.
Diddy: “See you later alligator!” Alligator: “At your trial pxdophile!”
“We’ll Be Right Back!”
There’s still time to turn back…
Not all heroes wear capes.
Reverse Diglett.
What was the plan here?
Instant karma
“I wonder what those holes are fo-“
“Slippery when wet.”
o no :c
Weeeeeee
This is better than sex.
Fus Ro AAAAAAHHHHHH
It was a calculated risk. He’s just bad at math.
Instant karma
When you don’t know the controls yet but you win anyways.
Hans. Get the Flammenwerfer.
Tony Hawks Ice Skater
Gnarly.
I’ll take brains over looks but here I am with neither.
Bro is lucky he didn’t break his neck.
Is it possible to be that close to a live wire and not have your massive brass balls conduct a current?
RIP in peace.
Buy a lotto ticket my mans.
She was horny. The bull was hornier.
You spin me right round baby right round
Car: Wrecked. Pants: Pooped. Driver: Alive. https://www.dailymail.co.uk/video/russia/video-2296240/Video-Driver-miraculously-survives-concrete-bridge-falling-car.html
POV: You all reached the end. I’m proud of you c:

item.dc:creator: danat101

item.title: There Is More to RPGs than D&D
item.description:
Hello everyone. It's been a fair while since my last overview of RPGs that are not D&D, so I think it's time for another one. As always, all of these are games that I have some personal experience with, though since my current situation doesn't really facilitate gaming much (since pretty much all of my players are five hours away), for many of these games I will not be providing any actual gameplay experience. And as before, my intention is not to convince you that D&D is bad (whatever my own opinions might be), but rather that the incessant attempts to twist and bend its ruleset to fit every possible setting and situation make it very poor fit for so many genres and settings that benefit from having games made specifically for them. So, whether you are looking to play a swashbuckling pirate of the Caribbean, a suave interplanetary woman of mystery, or a nine-to-five office drone with a secret life of magic, you are better off looking for a game among these.
If you are absolutely dead set on playing D&D, play Pathfinder instead. Now in second edition and with newly redesigned core books, this game offers the same kind of high-magic fantasy adventures as D&D but with a whole lot less jank. Just be careful, as the new redesign essentially replaces the original 2e core books, that you don't accidentally get the same content twice - the books you want to get are GM Core, Player Core, and Monster Core (and eventually Player Core 2, which should be coming out any day and should - I assume - contain classes that are inexplicably missing from Player Core, like the monk and the sorcerer).
Dragonbane is, at its core, a fairly typical D&D clone. It's a modern reimagined edition of Sweden's original RPG _Drakkar och Demoner_ that seems to have been as universally popular in Sweden back in the day as _Dungeons and Dragons_ was in the US (and _Dračí doupě_ in Czechia). Other than that, it is quite familiar to anyone with basic knowledge of fantasy and fantasy RPGs (and without the moronic Vancian spell slots), though anyone unlucky to consistently roll nat 1s might be interested in learning Dragonbane's system is a roll-under - the lower the roll the better.
Also, you can play Darkwing Duck.
The Burning Wheel is the grandfather of all modern narrative RPGs, but don't mistake "narrative" for "rules-lite". This game is **crunchy**. The Burning Wheel is also the game that out-Tolkiens D&D, with immortal Elves feeling Sorrow at the state of the world eventually wasting away and Dwarves hoarding gold with Greed. The BW is a game about the characters' Beliefs and the way the characters make the story. It was also one of the first games that strongly pushed the idea that the Game Master (or Dungeon Master, or Storyteller, or what-have-you) should never resolve a roll with a flat "No." Every roll, even if it ends in a failure, needs to drive the story onward, so a failure should always be at least a "no, but..." This is quite common in modern games, but when The BW came out in mid-aughts, it was revolutionary. If you want a crunchy mediaeval fantasy, play The Burning Wheel.
If, on the other hand, you want a real mediaeval fantasy, play Torchbearer. It's The Burning Wheel but dark. Your characters aren't heroes, they're outcasts, vagrants, the dregs of society. No sane person would leave their farm and delve into ancient crypts, no respected noble would even consider spending their nights in the freezing woods outside their castle. The system of Torchbearer is quite similar to The BW (they are written by the same guy, Luke Crane), though since this game is newer, it has had many years of playtesting and improvements.
Additionally, if you are interested in the style of game that The Burning Wheel and Torchbearer present, but like animals, you can try Mouse Guard, which is The Burning Wheel but with mice (based on the brilliant comic saga of the same name). But don't be fooled - just like its source, the game is quite serious, albeit adapted for a slightly younger audience. Unfortunately, I only own this game in Czech, so no English picture for you. Sorry.
If you really want to play as a mouse but don't want to spend the time learning all the detailed mechanics of Mouse Guard, you can try Mausritter. Part of the OSR movement, Mausritter is very rules-lite and approachable - the box contains tokens for every item and spell, so that the players can place them on their character sheets to track everything visually, the rules are filled with all kinds of random tables to quickly generate anything from a campaign map to a season-appropriate weather event, every creature has d6 variants to make each one unique...
If, however, you are yearning for a true, genuine, undiluted adventure in the Middle-Earth, you can give The One Ring a try. This game is officially licenced and fleshes out the region of Eriador (the part of Middle-Earth between the Great Sea to the west and the Misty Mountains to the east; the Shire and the lands around it, more or less) sometime between The Hobbit and The Lord of the Rings - the Enemy has returned and is slowly gathering forces in the East, while the elves head to the Grey Havens to travel West, making the lands of the once great kingdom of Arnor more dangerous than ever before, and even the Rangers can do little about it. One interesting idea that TOR has done is involving the downtime (the time between adventures) mechanically in the game - the Adventuring Phase involves, well, the adventure, and once it's done, the Fellowship Phase allows the characters to perform various undertakings that can help in further adventures. Think of the narrative structure of Frodo and company - Frodo, Sam, and Pippin travel to Crickhollow (Adventure Phase) where they rest (Fellowship Phase), then they and Merry travel through The Old Forest (Adventure Phase) and rest at Tom Bombadil's (Fellowship Phase), cross the Barrow Downs to Bree (Adventure Phase) and rest at the Prancing Pony (Fellowship Phase), then travel east with Strider (Adventure Phase) to recuperate at Rivendell (Fellowship Phase).
Just like pretty much every game with customised dice, you can still play The One Ring with a set of six d6 and two d12, though the custom dice are very pretty and include the special _lambe_ on the Success Dice (d6, one extra success on 6), as well as the Gandalf rune (replacing 12, for automatic success regardless of the roll) and the Eye of Sauron (replacing 11, for a value of zero) on the Feat Dice (d12). Above are two sets, white for the Free Peoples of Middle-Earth and black for the forces of the Dark Lord.
If your fantasy preferences steer towards a more Central European direction (and you played and enjoyed The Witcher 3), you will want to give The Witcher RPG a try. In fact, if you saved Ciri in _Wild Hunt_, defeated O'Dimm in _Hearts of Stone_, and helped Anna Henrietta in _Blood and Wine_, and still want more Witcher in your life, this is the game for you, and you will barely have to learn anything new. Yes, you can even craft your own weapons and brew your own potions and decoctions.
Do you like Final Fantasy? How about Dragon Quest? Or Chrono Trigger? Would you like to play them again? Fabula Ultima (roughly translated as "Last Fable", "Ultimate Story", or perhaps even "Final Fantasy") is a brand new game that brings all those good old 8- and 16-bit RPGs to tabletop. And just like the video games it emulates, it supports stories set in a high fantasy world (like, say, Final Fantasy IX), techno fantasy (FF VIII), and natural fantasy, with high fantasy and techno fantasy atlases (which serve both as guides on how to create a setting like that, as well as a list of example settings) already available.
As the cover helpfully informs, Legends of Avallen is an RPG inspired by Celtic Britain during the period of Roman settlement, specifically by those parts of Britain that remained outside of Roman control. While it may seem a fairly typical fantasy RPG, it does have a fair few interesting mechanics - unlike most RPGs, it doesn't use dice for resolution but a deck of playing cards, with the suit adding or subtracting depending on the type of task the player is trying to accomplish; characters don't start with a class (of which there are four - warrior, reaver, mystic, and mage) but with a profession (which represents a sort of "commoner class", things like bard, merchant, scribe, alchemist, ...) and only gain a class on second level, effectively roleplaying the characters' journey into becoming adventurers and heroes rather than leaving it for their background, and later even further specialising with a choice of one of many legendary paths - this makes every character distinct from each other; and finally, gaining levels is not just about gaining power, it represents steps on the character's story arc, with different sections introducing different rules - on level 4, the character commits to the life of adventure and gains the ability to use Resolve, which grants bonus on rolls, on level 7, the character enters the period of doubt and introspection, once again losing the ability to burn Resolve for bonus, on level 10, the character undergoes inner transformation into a heroic figure, changing a part of their personality that may no longer fit and regaining the ability to use Resolve, and finally, on level 13, the character is now widely recognised, either for being a paragon of virtue (and therefore no longer having flaws) or for being an anti-hero (who no longer has any virtues).
Exalted is a game that takes inspiration in both ancient legends of mighty heroes (from Gilgamesh to Heracles) and 90s/early 2000s anime, while consciously avoiding the Tolkienesque strain of western fantasy genre. It's a fantasy whose heroes perform feats of legends and where the word "impossible" just means "an Exalt hasn't tried doing it yet"... or so the lore would have you believe. The truth is, the second edition (which is the one I have) was mechanically so broken the authors had to create so many errata it became known as edition 2.5. The third edition, which is the latest, has toned down the power level of the characters significantly (and better not look at its crafting system; that way madness lies). Still, it offers a unique take on the classic fantasy tropes and is well worth getting into. Besides, as the saying goes, "In D&D, a starting sorcerer can get killed by a cat; in Exalted, a starting sorcerer can level cities."
But perhaps you are looking for fantasy even stranger than that, in which case I invite you to the Ninth World of Numenera, set in the far future year of 1 billion, built on the ruins of eight great civilisations of the past. Nanotechnology saturates the air, allowing the inhabitants of this world to perform magic. A settlement can be protected by a mystical barrier (a forcefield) and defended by fire-spitting gargoyles (robotic entities with bioengineed flamethrowers). After all, "any sufficiently advanced technology" and all that. It is a science-fantasy setting without historicity - you can have mediaeval-style monks who understand the solar system and enjoy indoor plumbing, horse nomads whose cuisine includes tortillas, or a Roman empire-like society with decidedly modern sensibilities about sexualities, genders, and gender roles.
Long time ago, the Master built the House and life was good. Some of the Little Folk came Inside, helping the Master and protecting His family, and became Boggarts, a good and honest folk, some of them sought protection from the Household Forces and were Born Out of the Spark, the Draft, and the Drain, and became Sprites, cunning and learned, while others remained in the tree Outside, received their wings from the Stars and became Fairies; and somewhere in the Farbeyond, the strange Folk of those strange lands made a Contract with a great and unnamed Force and became the Sluagh. Then, one day, the Master disappeared. After many years of war, a fragile peace was established between the four great nations - the Realm of the Fairies, led by Tsarina Arcadia Oberonovna, which controls the Dining Room and the Kitchen; the Hearth of the Boggarts, led by Governor William Hearthworth Jr, which controls the Living Room; the Free Dominions (or Freedomes) of the Sprites, led the scholarly Symposium and its first-among-equals leader Professor Werner Reinhardt, which controls the Master Bedroom, the Bathroom, and the Smaller Bedroom; and finally, the Horde of the Sluagh, led by Khan Capitano Rodomonte, which controls the Basement.
As you might have guessed from the write-up, Household is about playing the little folk, fae, brownies, or what-have-you in an abandoned house in a pseudo-Napoleonic era setting. Even if you are not interested in the idea, I would personally encourage you to get this book as it is absolutely *gorgeous*.
Set in a world that strangely resembles our own, 7th Sea is part D'Artagnan, part Captain Jack Sparrow. The newly-united island realm of Avalon faces internal struggles as the kings of Highland Marches and the Emerald Isle of Inismore aren't yet convinced the union will benefit their own people, the royalty of Montaigne is dining on pheasants and gold-plated honey-glazed hams while the population is left to eat grass, the once-proud nation of Eisen is still recovering from thirty years of religious war that left the country torn and the countryside roaming with monsters, and the king of Castille is all but powerless to stop the rise of power of the Vaticine Church - after all, no one expects the Castillian Inquisition. 7th Sea is a game of brave Heroes standing up against unabashed Villains. You could be a swashbuckling pirate sailing from the island of La Bucca or from one of the many islands of the Atabean, a fencer and a duelist navigating the treacherous waters of the Vodacce society, a musketeer in service of the Sun King himself, or a Hexe, a monster hunter from Eisen who gains their powers by eating parts of the monsters they hunt (the authors couldn't legally call them witchers).
Apart from the Europe-equivalent of Théah, there is a number of supplements that cover Heroes from other parts of the world. **Pirate Nations** is a broad supplement covering all the various sea-faring nations both near Théah and abroad - Aragosta in the Atabean sea, ruled by pirates, the Atabean Trading Company that's just the East India Company with the serial names filed off and is quite possibly the single most evil organisation in the world (and that's saying something, considering there is a canonical organisation unitying all the worst Villains), La Bucca which, just like the Azores, is an island conveniently both close to the mainland and far enough to be out of sight, the island of Jaragua in the Atabean, where the Ifrian slaves overthrew their masters and set up a free nation, Numa, the remant of an ancient powerful empire that at one point controlled much of Théah (and is rather similar to the last days of the Byzantium), and the natives of the Atabean, the Rahuri. **The Crescent Empire** covers the titular empire, broadly equivalent to the Ottoman Empire, from the nations of Anatol Ayh (Turkey), Ashur (Syria), and Persis (Persia), to the Sarmian Kingdom (Israel) and the Tribes of the 8th Sea (the tribes of the Arabian peninsula). **The New World** brings information about the southern part of the newly discovered western continent, with nations inspired by the Incas, the Aztecs, and the Mayans. **Lands of Gold and Fire** describe the nations of Ifri, roughly similar to Northern Africa, from Aksum and Khemet in the East through Manden and Maghreb to Mbey in the West. And finally, the latest supplement, **Land of 1000 Nations** describes the Woven Lands ot the north of the nations of **The New World**, from the many tribes and peoples of the Dawn Lands stretching from the frozen wastes of the far north, the confederacy of the Ono'Enohto'Yeh, the People of the Accords, to the swamps and bayous in the south where the people of the Sertepe Alliance live.
7th Sea: Khitai is a standalone game which is nonetheless fully mutually compatible with the core 7th Sea game. It focuses on the region of Eastern and Southern Asia, from India's analogue of Agnivarsa and Thailand's alternative of Nagaja through Fusō (similar to Japan) and Han (roughly equivalent to Korea) to Shenzhou (7th Sea's version of China) and Khazaria (inspired by Mongolia).
Ars Magica is the greatest resource on mediaeval society and culture masquerading as an RPG you will ever find. Apart from that, it is a game of mages in a world that is very close to our own history's Late Middle Ages (except the magic is real here). The system is very similar to the various iterations of the Storyteller and Storytelling systems in White Wolf's World of Darkness and related RPGs - which is not all that surprising, since Ars Magica was originally developed by White Wolf, and it shares the freeform approach to magic with Mage the Ascension and Mage the Awakening (in fact, before AM was sold off, Ascension was intended as a "modern day continuation of Ars Magica"). Unfortunately, in my opinion, the game suffers from an overabundance of rules and rolls. Virtually everything has its own custom rule (and if you include the supplements, those rules often end up with multiple subrules as well) and many traits further customise some rules - for example, basic spell casting has seven possible spell durations, from Momentary (the spell ends as soon as it's cast) through Sun (the spell ends at the next sunrise or sunset) all the way to Year (the spell lasts until the sunrise on the fourth solstice or equinox), but mages of the House Merinita who are inducted into the mystery of Faerie Magic have access to more fairytale-like durations, from Bargain (the spell lasts for as long as the target keeps the promise) to Year + 1 (the spell lasts for 366 days, 367 on leap years). However, Atlas Games have recently announced a planned kickstarter for a definitive revised version of the fifth edition for this autumn, so perhaps there will be some streamlining coming our way.
Legend of the Five Rings takes place in the world of Rokugan, a fictional version of feudal Japan, with the players taking the roles of samurai, the noble servants of the great lords of the Emerald Empire. They could be _bushi_ (warriors), courtiers, artisans, _shugenja_ (priests), monks, or even _shinobi_ (assassins and spies). The five rings then guide the characters' approach to things - do they act gracefully (Air) or passionately (Fire), are they steadfast (Earth) or flexible (Water), and what is their sense of spirituality (Void)? Magic is everywhere in Rokugan, from the _kihō_ arts of the monks and the invocations to the spirits of the _shugenja_ to the wicked and unnatural _mahō_ of the evil _mahō-tsukai_.
I know I mentioned that most custom dice systems can be played with normal dice. L5R makes that rather difficult, and you're better off just using a dice roller if you can't buy the official dice. In common with other Fantasy Flight Games products, the system is not really straightforward and some faces can include multiple results, so you could **succeed** with an extra **opportunity** but suffer some emotional **strife** in the process.
Maybe you'd like feudal Japan more if everyone was a furry. Usagi Yojimbo RPG is an adaptation of the well-known (and highly recommended) comic book by the same name, and (apart from the whole animal thing) is worth taking a look at for anyone interested in roleplaying in early 17th century Japan.
Lighthearted is a game inspired by 80s coming of age movies, music videos, and Saturday-morning cartoons. It's set in a world of neon lights powered by people's emotions and magic classrooms next to the newly-built computer lab, where you may not believe in fairies, but they certainly believe in you. It's a game of high emotions, as its system explicitly uses emotions in its resolution mechanics - you might be approaching a problem passionately, you might be feeling peaceful when dealing with a situation, or you might just be sad when you have to tell bad news to someone. Every emotion is associated with a colour, as is every type of magic (telekinesis is orange, which is associated with resolve and determination, while illusion magic is blue, the colour of confusion, surprise, and fear), which leads to the bright neon colours highlighting the whole game. And yes, as you might expect from the style, the game is quite queer and very queer-positive.
The three Star Wars games by Fantasy Flight Games are quite interesting in that they are fully mutually compatible, making it easy to play together an Edge of the Empire scoundrel, Age of Rebellion operative of the Alliance, and a Force and Destiny Force-sensitive character. All three games are set in between Episode IV and Episode V - after the Rebels destroyed the first Death Star but before the Empire attacked Hoth.
Another system with unique dice (it's Fantasy Flight Games, I'm telling you), you will typically be rolling a combination of positive (light blue, green, and yellow) and negative (black, purple, and red) dice, with some results cancelling each other out but some powerful results applying regardless, so you can easily end up with some combination of successes and failures (which cancel each other out) and advantages and threats (which also cancel each other out; these cause some extra good or bad effects in addition to the straight success or failure) - you could fail with an advantage, in which case you don't achieve your goal but something else good happens nonetheless, you could succeed with a threat, where you achieve your goal but something bad happens. The white die is the Force die, which doesn't directly produce results, but instead generates Light Side and Dark Side points a Force-sensitive character can use to activate their Force powers - but be wary of relying on the Dark Side too much...
Edge of the Rebellion characters are the scoundrels, the bounty hunters, and the colonists, living out on the edges of the civilised space. All EotR characters have an Obligation to someone - whether they have a bounty on their head, a debt to a powerful crime lord, or a family back home they need to take care of - and this Obligation can rear its ugly head when Jabba decides to send Greedo to collect.
Age of Rebellion characters are the ace pilots, the diplomats, and the spies, working for the Rebel Alliance. All AoR characters have a Duty to the Alliance, a way they can help out the cause that goes beyond the immediate mission - they might be on the lookout for any useful information to share, spreading the good word among the downtrodden, or liberating useful cargo from the Empire to send the Alliance's way - and fulfilling their Duty can help the characters get some help back from the Rebels, whether that's a safe house on Naboo or a small unassuming freighter that can slip past Imperial borders undetected.
Force and Destiny characters are the hidden Jedi padawans and the Force-sensitive children, learning about their heritage at a time their kind is hunted and exterminated. All FaD characters have a Morality, a balance between their emotional strength and weakness - a character might be moving between curiosity and obsession, ambition and greed, or bravery and anger - and letting it slip too low can lead the character to falling to the Dark Side.
But maybe your interests in the Star lie in another franchise (sorry, I don't own the Stargate SG-1 RPG). Star Trek Adventures, now in its second edition (completely fresh, the book came out mid-August), provides a complete ruleset for boldly going where no one has gone before (or to go initiate second contact before the locals start worshipping computers again), from the first flight of a warp 5-capable starship to the far future of the 32nd century, from away missions to starship combat.
Just like other Modiphius RPGs, Star Trek Adventures uses a version of their 2d20 system, with the player rolling at least two d20s against a target number made up of the combination of the appropriate attribute and department for the task (e.g. attempting a delicate surgery would require the **Control** attribute with the **Medical** department, while figuring out how to replace your subspace ionic relay with the artefact you found on your latest away mission would require **Insight** alongside your **Engineering**), and count any die that rolls under the target number as a success.
Star Trek Adventures: Captain's Log is a standalone conversion of the original Star Trek Adventures for a solo RPG play. Solo RPGs, popularised during the height of the pandemic, are RPGs designed to be played by just one person, though Captain's Log provides suggestions for cooperative roleplaying as well (essentially GM-less group roleplaying). The rules provide a system of random rolls to design everything, from a mission to a specific task, and it's up to the player to decide what exactly a success or a failure means in any given situation. Otherwise, the rules are largely similar to the 1st edition of STA.
Aah, the legendary Shadowrun. Pretty much anyone who's ever scratched the surface of cyberpunk roleplaying has heard of it. This is the game where a dwarf street samurai can join up with a troll shaman to fight against a toxic spirit summoned by an elven mage working for a multinational corporation, while their buddy controls a drone swarm to subvert the Matrix security of the building to rain bullets on its new unfriendly target. In short, it's cyberpunk with _magic_! So if this is something that interests you, _omae_, watch your back, count your bullets, and never deal with a dragon.
Interestingly for a licenced RPG, while the Blade Runner RPG is set (obviously) within the world of the Blade Runner movies, it sits in between the two of them, neither the 2019 of the original movie nor the 2049 of the sequel, but instead in 2037, shortly after the new Nexus-9 models, which promise new, safe s̶l̶a̶v̶e̶s̶ replicants, lead to the boom of legal replicants on Earth. Now, as Blade Runners of LAPD's Rep-Detect Unit, it is your job to ensure that humanity's safety is not compromised.
Blade Runner uses a fairly simple dice system, where anything above 6 is a success (which means that better attributes and skills, represented by bigger dice, have a better margin of success). The custom dice (from d6 to d12) just help by marking the good rolls with the eye symbol and are just pure vanity.
Dune: Adventures in the Imperium is the officially licenced adaptation of the Dune universe, taking inspiration both from the books and the movies. The game is by default set right before the House Atreides takes over Arrakis. You can play as members and agents of one of the many imperial houses, and the game allows for two different playstyles, called Agent and Architect - Agent playstyle is a typical RPG, where the characters are agents directly interacting with a situation, while in the Architect playstyle, the characters are in control of larger groups and organisations. The main difference is in the style and type of Assets (the game's representation of both tangible items and intangible relationships) - in an Agent playstyle, a character might have a Personal Shield asset that allows them to be protected during combat, while in an Architect style, the character might have access to a Facility Shield that allows them to protect an entire building under their control during large scale combat. And remember - fear is the mind-killer. Don't be afraid to play this game.
If you like Star Trek and Warhammer 40k, why not try combining them both in Rogue Trader? You can explore strange new worlds (and plunder their natural resources), seek out new life and new civilizations (who will surely be happy to buy trinkets from you in return for lots and lots of hard cash), and boldly go to where no man has gone ever since before the Imperium was established (surely these people will be more than happy to start paying you yearly tribute after so many years without the Emperor's light). In Rogue Trader, you play as the bridge crew of a Rogue Trader ship (including possibly the Rogue Trader themself), heading out beyond the Imperium's borders where the Rogue Trader's word is law. Your permit is broad - you can set up new colonies to spread the glory of the Imperium or rediscover old ones, you can busy yourself with establishing lucrative trade routes or load up a few companies and engage in the time-honoured tradition of enriching onself through warfare ("piracy" is such a crass word), or even get filthy rich by selling weapons to local conflicts (after all, if you sell to both sides, you'll always come out on top).
Yet another 40k RPG, Imperium Maledictum has you assume the role of an ordinary Imperial citizen, distinguished only by your service to a Patron, whether it's a captain of an Imperial voidship, Commissar of the Imperial Guard, or a member of the most holy Inquisition. As such, your characters may be sent on a mission to investigate a missing shipment in one of the sector's hive cities, ensure the workers stripping a derelict don't come across anything heretical, or purge a Chaos cult brewing in the bowels of a Chartist voidship.
Another one of the licenced RPGs, Doctor Who: Adventures in Time and Space allows you to play in the Doctor Who universe, whether you play as the Doctor and his companions, the Doctor's multiple regenerations, or even an independent teams ranging from UNIT and Torchwood to private investigators à la Sarah Jane Smith. One interesting feature of this system is its initiative resolution - you don't roll for initiative nor do you use a static number to figure out the initiative order. Instead, each player declares their intent (i.e. what they intend to do) and the order is determined by that - talkers go first (because as we've seen time and again, the Doctor is always able to talk his opponents down before any shots are fired), followed by movers (when the Doctor says "Run," you take the opportunity and **run**), doers (there is always time for jiggery-pokery), and at last by fighters. This way, the game leans heavily towards solving any conflict with means other than combat (as anyone who wants to do something else will get their chance to run away or hit the important button _before_ you get to shoot them).
War. War never changes. Fallout The Roleplaying Game is yet another in Bethesda's long line of attempts to pretend New Vegas doesn't exist and that if they keep talking about the Commonwealth for long enough, people will start thinking Fallout 4 was a good game. Because this game really is just Fallout 4: The RPG. But this time with a real-life human GM who can come up with a better plot hook than "where's my kid". Can you tell I'm not a huge fan of Bethesda's mutilation of a once-beloved game franchise?
And once again, another set of custom dice, with a fairly interesting resolution mechanic - you roll 2d20 (you can buy more dice by spending your Action Points) and each die that rolls under the target number is a success; however, the target number is not static like in some other games, it's determined by the attribute and skill you are trying to use, so that if you put most of your points in Strength and Melee Weapons, hitting someone with a club will be quite easy, but you might struggle with picking a lock with your pitiful Perception and Lockpick skill. The remaining dice are used for rolling damage (the d6s, with Vault Boy counting as a hit + extra effect) and to determine a random hit location, respectively.
Gran Meccanismo is set in a Renaissance Italy in a world of Leonardo da Vinci's imagination come to life. Florence, under the control of Niccolò Machiavelli and guided by the clockwork precision of da Vinci's Gran Meccanismo, a massive computational engine with terminals popping up all over the city, is flourishing and spreading the New Science to the rest of Italy and beyond. Your characters could be from one of the old Italian families, dealing with the sudden importance of brains over birthright, you could be one of the new clockers, who feed their custom designed punch cards into the Meccanismo, convincing it to change the facts to your liking, you could be bravos and soldiers always ready for a scrap, whether it's against another noble family or the enemies of Florence abroad, or you could even be engaged in the equally cuthroat and deadly business of commerce and banking.
New Angeles was supposed to be a scientific paradise, a new utopia for a new century, swiping away the tired old industrialism of the 19th century with the shiny brightness of the 20th. Instead, the Long War began and with it seventy long years of strife and destruction that saw most of the nations of the Earth in ruins. Now, it is the year 1984 and newfangled jazz music celebrates the ending of the war in the streets of **Tomorrow City**, a dieselpunk setting inspired by George Orwell's 1984, Fritz Lang's Metropolis, as well as the Bioshock games. While outside of the City, the world may be in ruins, on the inside, the 20s never stopped, with art deco stylings and heavy cars driving the streets, automatons filling up the factories and stranger science building wonders and horrors of the future. Your characters are Revs, professional troubleshooters, whether you're solving problems for the Ministry of Truth, helping out a faction intent on subverting the omnipresent government oversight, or hire out to the highest bidder.
Tales from the Loop is the Stranger Things and/or E.T. game you have been looking for but didn't know it. Set in the '80s that never was, this game is about a group of Kids investigating Mysteries in the shadow of one of the world's particle accelerators (The Loops) and the technology they brought about. It is the time when giant robots crossing the countryside and massive gauss ships gliding above the ground are *cool*, but not strange. But as your characters are kids, sometimes the most difficult part of the adventure may be convincing your parents that you will definitely do your homework as soon as you come back but now you really need to go see the cool thing your friends found. On the other hand, as the characters are children, it is entirely impossible in this game for the characters to die.
The dice mechanics of Tales from the Loop are simple - roll attribute + skill amount of dice, sixes succeed, and a single success is usually enough to succeed (with the exception of extremely difficult tasks). You can however spend extra successes to buy extra effects to your result, so rolling more sixes is always a good thing.
Things from the Flood is a companion game to Tales from the Loop. Where that game saw the players as kids in the '80s, in this one they are teenagers in the '90s. And although some of the world's problems has been resolved (the Cold War is over and no one has to be afraid of the Big One anymore), the strange technology has been going haywire, making the world a more dangerous place - robots no longer obey commands and sometimes turn feral, gauss ships appear thousands of kilometres off their course, and these new-fangled video games can literally suck people's lives out. As befits a more dangerous time and a more dangerous period in one's life, this game adds rules for permanent scars and even death (though it may not necessarily be a literal one - having a character removed from their family by the authorities and placed into a foster system halfway across the country is just as permanent).
The World of Darkness, nowadays more commonly known by its second edition title Chronicles of Darkness, is the baseline setting and ruleset for many urban fantasy games, ranging from Vampire the Requiem and Mage the Awakening to Changeling the Lost and Mummy the Curse. This game provides all the shared rules, as well as the rules for playing ordinary mortals in a world that is a dark reflection of our own.
Mage the Awakening is a gameline under the New World of Darkness/Chronicles of Darkness umbrella. Your characters are mages, people capable of achieving incredible reality-bending feats through their access to the true reality that was stolen from humans a long time ago. The mages are the only ones who know that the world around them is just a lie, a Plato's Cave reflection of the true world, and wish to bring the true reality back... although they might have some disagreements on how exactly it should be achieved. A fairly unique feature of this game is the flexibility of its magic system, as there are only a few hardcoded limitations on what magic can't achieve. Everything else is within the grasp of a dedicated group of mages (or even a single mage, if they are hardworking enough to do everything on their own).
The legendary Vampire the Masquerade, the game that was played by every goth roleplayer in the nineties, is now available in two versions - the 20th anniversary edition, which compiles all the rules of the classic VtM into a unified rulebook, and the fifth edition (usually shortened as V5), depicted here, which continues the overall metaplot of these games and changes both the rules and the setting. VtM is a game of vampires (surprise!) set in a world very similar to our own but much darker - secretive organisations control the world's society from the shadows and predators of all kinds (supernatural or not) lurk behind every corner (in fact, the original Vampire the Masquerade started the old World of Darkness setting). And although the player characters are vampires - predators - they are not exempt from the control of their elders (who might be hundreds, or even thousands of years old). If you decide to get into V5, I would heartily recommend grabbing at least the Players Guide along with the core book, as the main book only covers seven of the thirteen (well, fourteen, actually - it's complicated) vampiric clans (a clan is a group of vampires that can trace their origin to a single ancestor; they have common strengths and weaknesses and tend to have similar powers), while the Players Guide covers the remaining six+one. Other books that might be useful depending on your focus are the books on the individual organisations - **Camarilla** (the "default" group of vampires that believes that if humanity as a whole ever found out about vampires, they could wipe them out with ease, so it enforces a system called "Masquerade" to prevent any information exposure), **Anarch** (the Anarchs are vampires who rebelled against what they saw as oppressive control by the Camarilla; they still understand the importance of the Masquerade), **Sabbat: The Black Hand** (the Sabbat are vampires who see themselves as superior to humans and believe that the ancient ancestors of vampires are about to rise and eat everyone, so they practice a very Darwinist, survival-of-the-fittest approach to preparation for the inevitable apocalypse), and **Second Inquisition** (the Second Inquisition is a collective term for human organisations, both religious and military, that are dedicated to hunting vampires; they're always the bad guys in pretty much any Vampire game). Additionally, for those familiar with the classic VtM, there has been a general streamlining of the Disciplines (the vampiric powers), which led to some of the clans losing their unique Disciplines - for example, the Tzimisce no longer have Vicissitude; instead, they gain Protean, Vicissitude being just an amalgam power of Protean with Dominate - so you may want to consider the changes if you're deciding between V20 and V5.
Unknown Armies is a game similar to Mage the Awakening (or Mage the Ascension) but much... street and punk than those games. The characters in Unknown Armies perform magick (the "k" is important) through the power of their obsession, whether they are Adepts (who obsess over a specific thing, whether that's collecting books, cutting yourself, or getting shitfaced, and use that to do magick) or Avatars (who obsess over a specific archetype, like The Mother, The True King, or The MVP, and gain thematic powers the closer they resemble their archetype).
Rivers of London is a tabletop adaptation of the book series by the same name, using a slightly customised Call of Cthulhu system. You will play as new members of the Folly (a special unit of the Metropolitan Police dedicated to investigating crimes of a supernatural nature), whether official officers of the Met or civilian consultants, instructed in Newtonian magic as taught by DCI Thomas Nightingale and codified by DC Peter Grant, and investigate all sorts of paranormal and/or criminal activity in London and beyond - unless you decide to instead join the Abteilung KDA with Tobias Winter and Vanessa Sommer or the unnamed FBI department with Special Agent Kimberley Reynolds. In your work, you may encounter ghosts, other practitioners, and even the occasional _genius loci_ (spirits of a specific place, usually a river, from the eponymous Rivers of London to the Rhine Maidens).
Have you ever dreamed about being a secret ninja in modern-day Japan? With Shinobigami, you can play out your dreams. As an original Japanese RPG, the style is a fair bit different from many of the traditions established in western RPGs. The game progresses in a series of Cycles, during which each character gets to play out one Scene (either a dramatic scene or a combat one) before handing over the reins to the next character (other characters can of course join in, but only one of them is crucial for the scene); this ensures that every character gets equal place in the spotlight. Additionally, every character has a Secret they're trying to keep from the others, and finding it out is an important part of the gameplay. Combat initiative uses a so-called Velocity System - players secretly choose their position on the system, with the higher number meaning faster movement but also higher chance of an automatic roll, with the speeds ranging from Ghost Walk at the lowest (well, lowest for a ninja, there is a Mundane speed that's even slower) all the way to Light Speed. As you may have surmised, while this game can be played cooperatively, the heavy focus on learning other character's Secrets and finding out Information about them lends itself very well to a battle royale, everyone against everyone style of gameplay, with alliances forming and shifting as the situation changes. If that is something that interests you, I would give Shinobigami a try.
Do you like Supernatural? Of course you do. But do you like the old Supernatural, before the season-long arcs, when most episodes focused on the monster of the week? Then this is the game for you. This game allows you to play as Hunters just like Sam, Dean, or Bobby, driving around the country in your choice of vintage car, taking on ghost and ghouls and all sorts of goblins.
Cthulhu Hack is, as the name suggests, a game in the eldritch setting of Lovecraftian horror, where unknowable entities lurk beyond the thin layer of reality and reading a book may leave you a gibbering mess. Although the system it uses is different, the game takes clear inspiration from the classic Call of Cthulhu RPG, with the players taking on the roles of Investigators, delving into the mysteries of the universe and rarely escaping unchanged, or at all. Quite unique in this game is the system of Resources - Investigation, Sanity, and Supplies - which are checked after a situation warrants it and decrease on a failure. Direct success-or-failure rolls only happen as a reaction to danger, mundane or mythos. In this way, the characters will always succeed at finding something but may exhaust their Investigation resource until eventually they can't investigate anymore and need to rest and recuperate, and repeated encounters with strange entities and dangerous books can drain their Sanity to the point of breaking.
While I am sure I don't need to introduce Avatar (whether it's The Last Airbender or The Legend of Korra) to you, just on the off chance that people not familiar with the source are reading it - this game is set in a fantasy world inspired by East Asian cultures, from imperial Japan to the nomadic tribes of Siberia and Kamchatka, where some people, called benders, can control a specific element. And in each generation, the Avatar, who can control all four elements and serves a bridge between the physical world and the world of spirits. For people familiar with the shows, this game covers four separate eras, ranging from the times of Avatar Kyoshi (read the books, they're good), Avatar Roku, Avatar Aang, to the latest era of Avatar Korra. I picked this game as the first of several Powered by the Apocalypse (or PbtA) games to represent the system as a whole. These games are easily recognisable by their use of moves and playbooks. Moves are the core of the Apocalypse World system - they represent the intent of an action - is your character trying to **push their luck** by attempting something they've never done before, or are they **relying on their skills and training** to do something they've done a thousand times already? Every PbtA has its own selection of moves, which can tell you a lot about the general focus of that particular game - if the game has four moves about interpersonal relationships and only one related to physical attack, it's a safe bet that social conflict is a lot more important in that game than physical one. Playbooks are the "classes" of PbtA games, but they tend to be more general than you may be used to. You would expect for an Avatar game to have classes like "waterbender", "earthbender", "Avatar", "technologist", ... Instead, some examples of playbooks in this game are The Bold (who is certain of their greatness and works hard to prove it), The Icon (who was raised to fill an important traditional role and struggles over whether it's the right path for them), or The Prodigy (who knows they are better than everyone else and struggles with forming any bonds with people). Playbooks also give the character access to unique moves and, in some games, unique mechanics.
The basic roll of a PbtA game uses 2d6 + the relevant attribute (which usually ranges from -2 to +2). On a result of 7-9, you gain a partial success - you succeed but at a cost; maybe you don't learn everything you could have, maybe you need to spend some form of resources to achieve your goal, or maybe you and your opponent both succeed. Full success only occurs on a result of 10 or higher.
Monster of the Week is a game in the vein of Buffy Summers, the Winchester brothers, and agents Scully and Mulder, where a group of ordinary (and extraordinary) people protect the unaware public by hunting those who would hunt them. Because this game heavily encourages team-based play, there are even playbooks (in the Codex Worlds supplement) for your team as a whole, whether you are the working schmoes of the Night Shift, an elite commando unit of the International Monster and Paranormal Service, or part of the Interdimensional Crisis Team. Although the core premise of the game assumes largely paranormal monsters, they can have all kinds of origins - the Guardians of the Borderlands watch out for all kinds of fae beings entering their world through the gate they are sworn to protect, The Whistleblowers found out the organisation they worked for were the real monsters, and the Agents in Black have their hands full with all the illegal alien activity happening out in the country.
Masks: A New Generation (again, I only have the book in Czech) is one of the surprisingly rare superhero genre RPGs, inspired by shows and comics like Teen Titans, Young Avengers, Young Justice, Runaways, and Ms Marvel. In short, your characters will be the newest generation of teenage superheroes, fighting both villains and the weight of expectations. And the **teenage** part of "teenage superhero" is quite central to the game - your characters will have to deal with the adult supers sidelining them, grounding them, or not believing them that a supervillain threat is more important than it seems, they will not have the emotional maturity to deal with interpersonal conflicts in the healthiest way, they might have mentors who expect them to follow orders and not shame the legacy of someone whose name they're bearing, ... As such, this game does require a certain amount of player buy in and healthy handling of mature and emotional topics at the table.
Like Monster of the Week, Monsterhearts takes inspiration from (among others) Buffy the Vampire Slayer. But where MotW focuses on the fight, Monsterhearts focuses on the lives. Your characters are, quite literally, monsters - vampires and werewolves, ghosts and ghouls, witches and servants of Dark Powers. But above all, they are the worst monster of all - teenagers. Yes, Monsterhearts is a game about teenager monsters, with all the drama, heartbreak, and overblown emotions teenage life entails. Among the games on this list, this one shares the highest requirements of player buy-in - you can't just bring this game up to your players and expect them to go along. Especially since this game is _very_ queer (though not as much as the next one) - one of the basic moves, _turn someone on_, heavily encourages the players to let the game play out even if their character is turned on by someone they didn't expect - after all, teenage years is the time when the jock realises what was missing from his every relationship and the shy girl who wasn't interested in romance at all finds her heart beating just a bit faster when the cute barista smiles at her. Consequently, this game also features a full chapter on safety, consent, and boundaries in roleplaying (which has been referenced and even copied by other games), well worth the read for any RPG.
If you are dissatisfied with the levels of queerness in Monsterhearts and would much rather play a real gayme, then Thirsty Sword Lesbians is for you. If you ever imagined being defeated in a sword duel, only for your oponent to use the tip of her sword to lift your chin up to look into her eyes, then Thirsty Sword Lesbians is for you. If you were sad and angry when She-Ra and the Princesses of Power ended, then Thirsty Sword Lesbians is for you. Whether their battles take place in lush fantasy forests, shiny spaceships, or cozy cafés, the characters in this game should always be ready to fight the forces of toxicity and heteronormativity or embark on a whirlwind romance with a foe turned lover, and do so with style, panache, and many a buckled swash. And yes, this is another game that requires a heavy player buy-in.
And now, for something completely different, a game of playing out a Latin American telenovela, Pasión de las Pasiones. In this game, you play as characters in a telenovela, with all its melodrama, hightened emotions, and subtle magical realism - after all, where else can you return from the dead p̶l̶a̶y̶e̶d̶ b̶y̶ a̶ d̶i̶f̶f̶e̶r̶e̶n̶t̶ a̶c̶t̶o̶r̶ having undergone an experimental cosmetic surgery to take revenge on your brother who shot you to gain your half of the inheritance and married the love of your life? Where else can you be retroactively decide to have been your own twin all along while the real you plotted behind the scenes? And where else can you accomplish all this before the end of the first game session? This is also the last of the heavy player buy-in games, as your players will **really** have to want to play a Spanish telenovela.
Blades in the Dark is an evolution of the PbtA rules into something new and interesting. Your characters in this game are criminals and thugs in a city of Doskvol in a world plunged into darkness and shadows when the sun shattered a thousand years ago, protecting their turf from other gangs and planning the next big score that will make them all rich. And the **score** is the meat of the game, as much of the gameplay revolves around planning it before carrying it out. One very unique feature of the game is the system of flashbacks, where a character can retroactively decide during the score that they prepared just for the situations they found themselves in - maybe they arranged for a backup to arrive right when they get caught by the guards, or maybe the secretary was working for them all along. Another idea that BitD brought to the roleplaying table that has since been adopted by other games and can be easily used in pretty much any game are the **clocks**. A clock is a simple measurement of an action with multiple steps - you can use a clock to measure how much time is left before the enemy arrives or how many people in the crowd you need to convince to turn them into your mob. Some clocks progress on their own while other require actions or even rolls, or even failing a roll (e.g. for a clock tracking how close the guards are to raising an alarm, which progresses any time a character fails and causes some noise). Setting up a clock is easy - just draw a circle and divide it into four, six, or eight segments.
Scum and Villainy is a Forged in the Dark (FitD, i.e. based on the rules of Blades in the Dark) about a group of scoundrels and ne'erdowells trying to keep the ship flying while the tyrannical Galactic Hegemony makes it difficult for everyone. You might be smugglers, always on the move to stay one step ahead of the authorities, bounty hunters following their prey through star systems, or rebels fighting the Hegemony with guerilla strikes. Some characters might even be attuned to The Way, a mystical field of energy that surrounds and penetrates every living being and is definitely different from a mystical field of energy that surrounds and penetrates every living being in a galaxy far, far away. If you think this game sounds like a cross between Firefly and Star Wars, you wouldn't be entirely wrong.
The Ultimate Micro-RPG Book offers a whole slew of simple two-page games that run the gamut from comedy to drama, from office politics to the music business, from the land of the gnomes to star-spanning empires. Every game is unique with its own mechanics. Some games are GMless. Some are suited for large social groups, others can't be played with more than two people. Some are simple, others are more complex (though since all the rules have to fit onto two pages, not overly complex). You should get this just so you can experience the sheer variety of roleplaying genres and approaches. If you are not willing or able to get this book, you can also check out the various small games from Bully Pulpit, available as free download from their website (just as an example, _The Hot Lamp_, _The Last Train Out of Warsaw_, or _We Are Here to See the Evil Wizard Kormákur_).
Genesys is a generic roleplaying system which originated in the Fantasy Flight Games' Star Wars games (see above), with the SW-specific rules filed off. There have also been several setting-specific supplements published, like Realms of Terrinoth (a high fantasy setting), Android: Shadow of the Beanstalk (a cyberpunk setting), or Embers of the Imperium (a space opera setting), with each setting originating from another game published by FFG.
Just like the Star Wars games, Genesys uses a set of custom narrative dice, which often generate complex results (success with a cost, mitigated failure, ...). There is no Force die for obvious reasons, though.
Fate (or Fate Core, as its latest edition is known) is another well-known generic system (with several custom settings available, like Venture City - in the photo - offering a superhero setting). Core to the Fate system are Aspects, which describe the characters (a character can have Gained a Military Distinction, they can be a Beloved Apprentice of the High Priest Alaro, or they may have Lost His Leg in a Car Crash), but also the situation in a scene (the place may be On Fire, there might be Lazy and Bored Guards, or the characters might find themselves in an Old Mansion Filled with Secret Passages), which can be both invoked to provide an advantage or compelled to complicate the situation. And this use of Aspects ties into another formerly unique feature of Fate, the Fate Points; these are metagame currency which can be spent to improve a roll or provide some non-mechanical benefit and are gained whenever a player willingly makes their character's situation more difficult, usually due to their some trait of the character (in Fate, this is compelling an Aspect). The idea of Fate Points proved so popular since the game first came out that nowadays, many games have implemented it as well, including multiple games on this list (look for things like Destiny Points, Karma Points, or similar).
Fate uses the Fudge dice for its resolution mechanic, a set of four dice with two plus and two minus symbols on the sides, so the final result will be anywhere between -4 (four minuses) and +4 (four plusses).
Index Card RPG is a generic system based on many years of mutilating and twisting the classic d20 system (i.e. what D&D uses) into something quite a bit different. It offers an example selection of five different worlds, from a traditional fantasy of Alfheim, space opera of WARP SHELL, weird western afterlife of Ghost Mountain, street-level superheroes of Vigilante City, to the frozen survivalist realm of Blood and Snow. However, to be perfectly honest with you, this game is still too much stuck in its d20 origins and, for a game that purports to offer a fresh mindset to GMs and players alike, its advice is still stuck in the same roleplaying style I'm not overfly fond of. However, if you prefer the style of games where the players are only active when they have to roll a die, letting the GM narrate everything else that happens in the world, this game might be your choice of a generic RPG.
FIasco is a game of failures, inspired by (among others) the Coen brothers' movies. It's a GM-less game with extremely simple rules, just a step up above a typical board game (and as such, it is one of my favourites to use for first-time players, as the game focuses on the roleplaying over any complicated mechanics). The game offers a large number of playsets (individual settings, whether it's Rainbow Mountain, about a peaceful commune far outside the hustle and bustle of city life that's definitely not a cult, The Ice, set at he McMurdo Station in Antarctica, where people getting on your nerves can lead to murderous actions, or the newly reopened Camp Death, where the murders 20 years ago will absolutely not have any impact on the present) in the form of a pack of cards, allowing for a random draw to quickly create your characters. These are not defined, as is common in other RPGs, by stats or skills or traits, but instead by the relationships between each neighbouring character. As such, your character could be Mother and Son with the character on your right and Witnessed a Mob Hit with the character on your left, who is Secretly Having an Affair with the character on their left, and so on. In addition to a Relationship, every two characters can have a Need (it could be a Need to Get Even Because They Stole Your Most Precious Object, Need to Get Out Before They Find Us, or Need for Respect from the One Who Hurt You the Most), a Location (whether it's Under the Bleachers with Missy Chambers, In the Secret Attic, or At the Duchess's Dinner Table), or an Object (maybe a Bag with Ten Thousand Dollars, Volleyball with a Bloody Handprint, or Map of New York Underground 1673), all of which together determine the group and the way the story goes. The game itself progresses through a series of Scenes, with each player getting their turn to shine. For their scene, each player can either decide to Establish or Resolve the scene, with the other players collectively taking the other action - the player who Establishes can set the start of the scene, whereas the player who Resolves can decide if the scene is ultimately good or bad for the character - establishing a scene ensures you have control over how the scene starts (the location, other characters, the situation), but the other players can decide it goes poorly for you depending on playing out the scene, whereas resolving it means your character will usually start in *medias res* of a sticky situation, but you have control over whether you can get something out of the scene or if your character's heads full speed towards a brick wall. As I mentioned previously, this game is absolutely brilliant for first-time players and lots of fun for experienced roleplayers. Get into it and don't be afraid of failure - in one of my games, my character died in a hail of bullets during her last stand, and it was glorious.
Alternatively, you can also get the classic version, which uses dice and written-out cards for its mechanics, but is otherwise the same game.
As other games in this section, Not the End is a generic game focused on telling dramatic stories. It is a game with a lot of really interesting ideas and, in my opinion, really terrible execution. It has a unique character creation - characters are not defined by stats and skill values, but instead by interconnected traits - that overcomplicates mechanically what would fit a rules-lite narrative game better. It has a unique character progression - among other options, characters can learn a lesson - except the lessons are static and predefined, since they have effects on the game mechanics. Even the character's death, which many other RPGs leave as a freeform, "your last and best moment to shine" for the player and the character, is constrained by the mechanics. Pretty much the only unambiguously good part of the mechanics is the resolution system, which uses two-coloured tokens in an opaque bag - white for the character's traits that are useful for the task, black for the difficulty, and the player can draw anywhere between one and four tokens to determine their success, and the tokens don't cancel each other out, which makes most "rolls" (draws?) result in both success and complications.
Flabbergasted sees you playing members of a social club in the roaring 1920s, inspired by the works of P.G. Wodehouse and Agatha Christie, among many others. You could be an old-money Aristocrat, part of the working Staff, an upper-middle class Well-To-Do, or even a free-spirited Bohemian, but together with the other members of your club, you will set out to make your club the best there is among all the other clubs (especially those wazzocks from the Horticulture Afficionados), whether that's hosting the most popular soirée of the season, solving the case of Lady Womberley's missing poodle, or beating those polrumptious Horticulturalists at a race to Upper Middlesborough with one of these new-fangled "automobiles".
The Darkest House is not quite an RPG (although it has its own rules system for the play in the House) and not quite a setting, either. Rather, it's an accompaniment for any RPG and any campaign, with the characters (most likely already established in their own game and setting) finding a strange house in their world and entering inside. Once inside, the characters change (are converted into the House System) and have to find their way back out again from a place that actively hates them. It is a haunted house distilled to its core. There is no restless ghost that can be put to rest. At best, the characters can hope to sever whatever ties bind the House to their world... if they survive their time on the inside first.
The Undying Sands is a unique RPG setting which you can assemble as you play, drawing the individual hexes as the party travels the dunes, encountering weird ruins and strange places. It can be paired with any RPG, though games that are less tied to a specific setting and which can include both weird magic and weird science (like e.g. Numenera or Thirsty Sword Lesbians) are better suited for this.
Just like the Undying Sands, Bottled Sea is a modular setting, this time in a Waterworld-like environment. The world itself is generated randomly by drawing cardboard hexes from the bag to assemble them as the party travels from one place to another.
A full box of hex tiles to generate any campaign map, Hexcrawl Toolbox is another product by Games Omnivorous (and is fully compatible with The Undying Sands and Bottled Sea). Unlike those two settings, however, this one intends to be fully generic (at least within the fantasy/weird sci-fi genre), with eight different biomes (ranging from ocean and pastoral to jungle and swamp) and four different ways of playing - campaigns with set maps (two examples are shown in the photo of the total four included in the box, but the GM can easily prepare their own custom one), random regions (the game includes a tearable pad of region sheets with randomly generated maps, which can be fitted with any biome), hex-in-a-bag (the same principle as used in the other two settings, the players draw random tiles from the bag each time they enter a new hex), and unknown regions (the map is set beforehand with only the backsides showing, and the players can turn the hex tile they're currently on to potentially reveal an encounter).
The Ultimate RPG series of books offers a wide variety of advice for both players and GMs on how to make your gaming session more engaging and interesting. **The Ultimate RPG Campfire Card Deck** is a deck of 150 cards with questions that can help quickly flesh out your character, ranging from "What was the worst job you ever had?" to "What organization do you admire but could never join?" These can help with making the character feel part of the world, with history that doesn't require 20 pages of written backstory, and can help the players be more comfortable with adding their own imagination into the game sessions (as opposed to passively sitting back while the GM does all the heavy lifting). **The Ultimate RPG Gameplay Guide** is the basic guide to all things RPG, from thoughts on how to prepare for a session (for players and GMs alike) and how to approach creating characters and the world, to the way both the GM and the players can help improve their game. **The Ultimate RPG Character Backstory Guide** and **The Ultimate RPG Character Backstory Guide: Expanded Genres Edition** offer multiple options and activities fors fleshing out your character's backstory, whether they're a fresh-faced adventurer or a living legend. The first book focuses primarily on fantasy genres, while the Expanded Genres Edition adds genres such as sci-fi, horror, and western, as well as some advice for any RPG. **The Ultimate RPG Game Master's Guide** focuses on the GM and offers a wide variety of advice, from the basic definitions and tips of table safety and conflict resolution to how to set up your game and how to run it. **The Ultimate RPG Game Master's Worldbuilding Guide** provides a number of questions and options for a GM to consider when creating their own setting, with focus on fantasy, sci-fi, horror, and X-punk (i.e. cyberpunk, steampunk, biopunk, ...) genres. However, be aware that there is certain amount of overlap between these guides, so you might be better off just picking one you're most interested in before delving into the rest.
And a cat tax as a reward for going through this whole thing.
item.dc:creator: InTheBeginningWasTheNerd

item.title: Car engine won’t turn over? How to fix?
item.dc:creator: tampacl

item.title: Thursday unhinged meme dump of pure chaos #259
item.description:
1. Yes, Mr. Anderson... COME with us
2. An average day in the life of a plumber
3. Thanks bro
4. Winding her up
5. He thought he could just smile and wag tail and get away with it... Narrator: He did.
6. Do Mortal Kombat and backpipes mix? You be the judge
7. Twerky twerky little frog
8. I'm really curious what he did at the job interview to prove he can do this job
9. How to hold gun in Italy
10. A magnificent shark
11. This messed me up for a sec
12. Futuristic chariot
13
14. Me, when I hear there is more pizza
15. Would you eat some nihilist pancakes? P.S. Thanks to the two people (Hi, Mom and Dad!) who made it to the end of this dump. When I'm not busy making questionable life choices on the internet, I've been pouring my soul into a little passion project. If you got a kick out of this dump, you're going to love my game: "Do Not Press The Button (To Delete The Multiverse)." Think "The Stanley Parable" but with even more chaos and quirky characters such this little nihilist pancake. Can you imagine? A first-person game mixed with humor? Anyway, please wishlist: https://store.steampowered.com/app/2336120/Do_Not_Press_The_Button_To_Delete_The_Multiverse/ It would mean the world to me and my brother if you checked out the game and gave it a wishlist. Anyway, that's all for now. Have a great Thursday. If work sucks, refer back to this dump and Imgur
item.dc:creator: TheseusGames

item.title: He gives NOOOO Shits
item.description: Actually... I think he's just tired...
item.dc:creator: VirEline
